3A_Bibliographic.citation: Gray, J. C., Schvey, N. A., & Tanofsky-Kraff, M. (2020). Demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive correlates of BMI in youth: Findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study. Psychological Medicine, 50, 1539-1547.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: ABCD Release Notes 1.0 (first wave, released 2018, N=4524 participants)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Participants were derived from the first wave of the ABCD study, an ongoing project seeking to enroll 11,500 9-10 year olds to assess longitudinally for 10 years. The first wave of data was released in 2018 and included 4524 participants. From the 4524 participants who completed the baseline assessment, 3847 survived quality control procedures.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Training sample n=2724, test sample n=1123. Linear regression on full sample N=3847.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time (average minutes per week on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device) - one of 43 predictor variables
3C_Exposure.modalities: Total screen time (computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous; average minutes per week; square root transformed due to skew
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was defined as the average minutes per week a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is ONE of 43 predictors entered simultaneously in machine learning models predicting BMI. It is not the sole or primary exposure.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Body mass index (%BMIp95 - percentage of the 95th BMI percentile)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Anthropometric measurement (height/weight measured twice); BMI calculated, converted to z-scores via CDC 2000 Growth Chart; converted to %BMIp95
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Height and weight were measured twice consecutively and the average of the two measurements was used. BMI (kg/m2) was calculated according to convention and converted to a z-score using the CDC 2000 Growth Chart SAS software. We converted these scores to the percentage of the sex- and age-specific 95th BMI percentile (the cutoff for obesity). This metric is referred to as %BMIp95.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple machine learning regression approaches: ridge regression, LASSO, elastic net, linear regression; 70/30 training-test split with 10-fold cross-validation; FDR correction for linear regression on full sample
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: 43 variables simultaneously entered including demographics (sex, race, ethnicity, income, marital status, parent education, single parent home, neighborhood safety), psychological/behavioral (8 CBCL subscales, screen time, exercise, sleep initiation, impulsivity facets, BIS/BAS, delayed reward discounting, FES conflict), cognitive (NIH Toolbox tasks, WISC-V matrix reasoning), and stimulant medication
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple machine learning regression approaches were utilized to attempt the best model prediction of %BMIp95. These approaches, all implemented using the R packages glmnet and caret, included ridge regression, least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) regression, elastic net regression, and linear regression. We also conducted a linear regression on the full sample (N=3847) with false-discovery-rate (FDR) correction of the 43 predictors.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Screen time is one of many predictors; this is a variable importance study, not a focused screen time study.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: LASSO coefficient from full sample training set (n=2724); ranked 10th of 25 non-zero coefficients. Positive = more screen time associated with higher %BMIp95.
3F_Effect_Sizes.r2_or_variance_explained: R2=0.12 for full LASSO model (25 variables)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Fig. 1: Screen time coefficient = 1.17 in LASSO full sample training set

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Screen time (sqrt transformed) - FDR-corrected linear regression
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Screen time was the second most significant variable after Hispanic ethnicity in the FDR-corrected linear regression on full sample (N=3847)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The linear regression on the full sample (N=3847) is broadly consistent with the findings from the LASSO model, with 11 variables surviving FDR-correction, including screen time as the second most significant variable (p=7.5x10-8) after Hispanic ethnicity

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: LASSO coefficient from female training set (n=1271); R2=0.12

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Screen time - male elastic net model
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Elastic net coefficient from male training set (n=1453); R2=0.10

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Screen time - Black non-Hispanic subsample elastic net
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Elastic net from Black non-Hispanic subsample (n=366); R2=0.03
R2 for full LASSO model in both training (n=2724) and test (n=1123) sets
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The LASSO model exhibited the lowest RMSE on the training set (RMSE=15.97, R2=0.12), generalized well to the test set (RMSE=17.34, R2=0.12)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: the primary analyses found only 25 of the original 43 variables to be associated with %BMIp95, and many of those had negligible effects... the limited variance accounted for suggests that many of the target variables may not generalize
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The full model with 43 variables explains only 12% of variance in %BMIp95. The paper acknowledges many variables have negligible effects but still highlights screen time as modifiable and important. Machine learning coefficients are not directly interpretable as traditional effect sizes.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: it may be beneficial for health care providers to encourage youth and primary caregivers to limit or alter screen time to emphasize video games that incorporate physical activity

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: this study highlighted both modifiable variables that are implicated in BMI (e.g. screen time, social problems) irrespective of sex and also variables that are not particularly relevant to BMI in this sample
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion positions screen time as one of several modifiable risk factors for childhood BMI, alongside social problems and reward responsiveness. The paper uses machine learning to rank variable importance rather than focus on any single predictor. Screen time is described as a consistent correlate across sex and ethnic subgroups. The 12% total variance explained is acknowledged as modest. The framing of screen time as modifiable justifies clinical recommendations despite the relatively small contribution of screen time to the overall model.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Machine learning coefficients (LASSO/elastic net) are penalized and not directly comparable to traditional regression coefficients. Screen time is one of 43 predictors; this is not a focused screen time study. The LASSO coefficient of 1.17 is on a square-root-transformed predictor scale making interpretation non-straightforward. CIs are not reported for individual predictors.
